dialogue.utterance: Unit 6, respond for a 72-year-old female with abdominal pain at the care facility on Broad Street.
Hi, I'm May, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She's been having lower right abdominal pain for five days, and staff noticed she's been nauseated, though she hasn't vomited in two days.
Thank you. Ma'am, can you tell me where exactly the pain is and if it has changed at all over the past few days?
Yes, the pain is in my belly, down on the right side. It feels constant and increases when I press on it, but it hasn't gotten worse than yesterday.
From the doorway, you appear A&Ox4. Let's confirm. What is your name?
It's Margaret.
What county are we in today, Margaret?
I believe it's Richmond County.
And can you tell me, what event led to calling for help today?
I just had more pain today, and the staff thought I should get help now.
Your eyes are open and alert. Can you follow my finger side to side when I move it slowly?
Sure, looks easy.
Good, and can you tell me what day it is today, Margaret?
It's Thursday, I think.
And on my verbal command, can you squeeze my finger gently with your right hand once if able?
Okay, done.
You're not having trouble breathing, and your airway looks clear. Have you experienced any breathing difficulties or choking recently?
No, not at all. Just a stomach issue, like I said.
Breathing looks unlabored and steady. Let's assess your breathing and pulse.
Her respiratory rate is 48 per minute. Fast, but not labored at this point.
Pulse is 86 beats per minute, consistent and strong bilaterally.
Blood pressure is 128 over 74; stable and within normal limits.
Oxygen saturation is 98% on room air, and her glucose is 98 mg/dL - no signs of hypoglycemia.
Respiration is staying at 48 - high, but not distressing with current clinical status.
Pulse remains at 86 - regular, no sudden changes observed.
Blood pressure is 127 over 69 now, still stable and normal.
Saturation has dropped slightly to 96%, but it's still good with room air. No signs of respiratory compromise currently.
Blood glucose remains at normal levels, no medications taken according to facility staff.
Can you tell me more about the abdominal pain you've felt, Margaret? Where, when, and what kind of pain?
It's in my lower right side. It feels like a constant ache, gets worse when you touch it.
Are there any other symptoms, besides pain and nausea?
No fever or chills. No vomiting for two days now, just some earlier diarrhea.
How many days ago did this pain start becoming noticeable, Margaret?
It was five days ago, from the start of the week.
How would you describe that ache? Sharp, dull, burning?
A dull ache. Sometimes I feel movement in my gut, but not cutting or anything.
Has your pain ever moved from the area you're pointing at?
No, it stays right in that lower right belly region.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now, with 0 being no pain and 10 being the worst possible?
Maybe a 4 or 5. Not the worst I've had in a while.
Has the intensity changed over the past five days?
It stayed the same really. I ignored it before, thinking it normal. But yesterday I felt it differently and told the staff I needed help.
We need to check your belly now. Please move your hands aside so we can do a quick exam, okay?
Tenderness is present in the RLQ. No guarding or rigidity noted previously.
No burns, rashes, or bruises on skin we can see. Have you noticed any on your body?
No, all looks fine. No injuries I can recall at this time.
Just to check in again, can you confirm the date?
Today is Thursday the 27th and it's February.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam (pupillary reflex / Size)
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam (pupillary reflex / Size)
dialogue.utterance: Good pupillary response, all signs are normal.
We note you have allergies to Celexa, Codeine Phosphate, Demerol, Iodine or IV Contrast Dye, and Morphine Sulfate. Is that accurate?
Yes, all of those listed.
You list in your medical summary that you currently are not taking medications. Is that still accurate?
That is correct - I may take some by facility recommendation but it's not fully on record.
Let me confirm your medical history: as listed in your facility records, you've had a history of major depression, and不同类型of dementia - one with behavioral disturbance, one without?
Yes, it's been a long ongoing issue with my mental health, and I've had to get support for severe depression and memory loss.
Do you recall what you ate or drank in the past 24 hours, Margaret?
Well, on the last few days I've had little to eat - just some tea and small meals for fear of stomach upset.
What made you and the staff decide it was time to call 911?
The staff said the pain didn't improve and I was getting nausea, so they decided I needed more especially.
Our field impression includes abdominal pain likely related to your GI system. We'll remain keen for signs of a potential abdominal aortic aneurysm based on your age and history.
Setting up the 4-lead ECG.
Has the 12 lead been completed yet?
Yes, showing normal sinus rhythm.
Great, no signs of cardiac issues detected so far.
This case corresponds to medical protocol 3-2, for abdominal pain with no indicators of acute circulatory compromise or cardiac involvement.
We'll be taking her to Henrico Doctors Hospital - Forest, as per protocol and staff request.
Stretcher secured in semi-Fowler's position, the patient is ready for transport.
If you start to feel any changes in pain or feel weak, just let us know, Margaret.
I will stay up and try to be still. Thank you.
BP is 131 over 70 - within normal limits and stable.
Respirations are 16 per minute - easier than before but still steady.
Pulse is now 82 - steady and regular.
Oxygen saturation is still 96% on room air.
Blood glucose remains in normal range.
Margaret, did you have any recent fever or changes in bowel or urination habits?
Only had some diarrhea four days ago, nothing after that. No fever or hot flashes.
She reported no fever during our assessment. Her only symptoms are abdominal pain and nausea without vomiting.
ECG was stable again - normal findings throughout the ride.
She remains alert with a GCS of 15 and shows no signs of mental decline during the transport.
We'll continue to monitor her for changes in pain, the presence of fever, or any signs of possible abdominal aortic aneurysm.
She has refused Zofran, and we will not be using it as per her prior statement.
Keep us posted if you feel faint, get chills, or sense any worsening during the trip, Margaret.
Alright, I'll tell you if anything changes.
EKG remains clear and the ECG monitoring gives a normal sinus rhythm while still on transport.
No changes in protocol as per reassessment - patient shows no signs of deterioration.
All systems are green and she shows no signs of fever or weakness. Transferring care at the destination should be smooth.
